author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
title: Volition and Ability.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Some one will ask whether the faith itself, in which seems to be the beginning either of salvation, or of that series leading to salvation which I have just mentioned, is placed in our power.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: We shall see more easily, if we first examine with some care what “our power” means.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Since, then, there are two things,—will and ability; it follows that not every one that has the will has therefore the ability also, nor has every one that possesses the ability the will also; for as we sometimes will what we cannot do, so also we sometimes can do what we do not will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: From the words themselves when sufficiently considered, we shall detect, in the very ring of the terms, the derivation of volition from willingness, and of ability from ableness.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Therefore, even as the man who wishes has volition, so also the man who can has ability.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But in order that a thing may be done by ability, the volition must be present.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: For no man is usually said to do a thing with ability if he did it unwillingly.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Although, at the same time, if we observe more precisely, even what a man is compelled to do unwillingly, he does, if he does it, by his volition; only he is said to be an unwilling agent, or to act against his will, because he would prefer some other thing.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: He is compelled, indeed, by some unfortunate influence, to do what he does under compulsion, wishing to escape it or to remove it out of his way.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: For if his volition be so strong that he prefers not doing this to not suffering that, then beyond doubt he resists the compelling influence, and does it not.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: And accordingly, if he does it, it is not with a full and free will, but yet it is not without will that he does it; and inasmuch as the volition is followed by its effect, we cannot say that he lacked the ability to do it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: If, indeed, he willed to do it, yielding to compulsion, but could not, although we should allow that a coerced will was present, we should yet say that ability was absent.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But when he did not do the thing because he was unwilling, then of course the ability was present, but the volition was absent, since he did it not, by his resistance to the compelling influence.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Hence it is that even they who compel, or who persuade, are accustomed to say, Why don’t you do what you have in your ability, in order to avoid this evil?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: While they who are utterly unable to do what they are compelled to do, because they are supposed to be able usually answer by excusing themselves, and say, I would do it if it were in my ability.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: What then do we ask more, since we call that ability when to the volition is added the faculty of doing?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Accordingly, every one is said to have that in his ability which he does if he likes, and does not if he dislikes.
